heroOverview: Age-related macular degeneration destroys the macula — the 5 mm central region of the retina responsible for sharp, colour, and detail vision — and is the leading cause of irreversible vision loss in people over 50 in high-income countries, accounting for roughly 8.7% of all blindness worldwide. The dry form, characterised by slow accumulation of fatty drusen deposits under the retinal pigment epithelium, affects about 85-90% of cases and progresses over years; the wet (neovascular) form, in which abnormal blood vessels leak fluid under the retina, accounts for the remaining 10-15% but is responsible for more than 80% of cases with severe central vision loss. Anti-VEGF injections into the eye have transformed wet AMD from a rapidly blinding condition to one that can be stabilised and, in many patients, improved. Early dry AMD causes no symptoms and is detected only on examination; the first noticeable sign is usually distortion of straight lines or a blurred spot in the centre of vision.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 196 million people worldwide (2020 estimate); projected to reach 288 million by 2040 (Wong et al., Lancet Global Health 2014)
keyStats.demographics: Prevalence rises sharply with age: less than 1% under 55, approximately 11% at 70-74, and above 27% in those over 85
keyStats.avgAge: Diagnosis most common after age 55; advanced disease peaks after age 75
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 8.7% of all blindness globally; 3rd most common cause of severe visual impairment worldwide

definition: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD; ICD-10: H35.3x) is a chronic progressive degenerative disease of the macula, the central portion of the retina that mediates high-resolution central vision. The underlying pathology involves accumulation of extracellular deposits called drusen between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch's membrane, progressive RPE dysfunction and atrophy, and in some cases growth of abnormal choroidal blood vessels that breach Bruch's membrane and leak blood and exudate into the subretinal space. AMD is classified as early (small to medium drusen with or without pigment changes, no vision loss), intermediate (large drusen or extensive medium drusen, possibly mild vision loss), and late (geographic atrophy or neovascular AMD, significant central vision loss). The disease belongs to geriatric ophthalmology and retinal medicine. Pathogenesis involves an interplay of age-related oxidative stress, lipid dysregulation, complement system dysregulation (particularly variants in complement factor H), and local inflammation — the ARMS2/HTRA1 and CFH gene variants are the strongest genetic risk factors identified. Peripheral vision is generally preserved throughout the disease course, so AMD rarely leads to complete blindness but severely impairs daily activities dependent on central vision such as reading, driving, and face recognition.

typesClassification.description: Characterised by medium drusen (63-125 µm diameter) without late AMD lesions. Typically asymptomatic; detected on routine examination. Annual conversion rate to late AMD is approximately 0.5-1%.

Large drusen (>125 µm) and/or geographic atrophy not involving the central fovea, or extensive medium drusen. May cause mild vision difficulty. Risk of progression to late AMD at 5 years is approximately 18% with the AMD Eye Disease Study classification.

Well-demarcated areas of RPE atrophy expanding toward the fovea over years. No licensed treatment beyond nutritional supplements (AREDS2 formula) at present, though several drugs targeting complement are in late-phase trials.

Choroidal neovascularisation breaches Bruch's membrane, leaking fluid and blood. Causes more rapid and severe central vision loss than dry AMD. Treated with intravitreal anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, aflibercept, brolucizumab, faricimab).

primarySymptoms: A blurred or missing spot in the central visual field (central scotoma), characteristically bilateral but often asymmetric, that worsens over months to years in dry AMD or over days to weeks in wet AMD.
Metamorphopsia — distortion of straight lines (door frames appear wavy, text lines curve), one of the earliest and most specific symptoms of wet AMD, assessed with an Amsler grid.
Difficulty reading fine print despite adequate lighting and corrective lenses, arising from loss of foveal photoreceptors rather than any refractive error.
Reduced contrast sensitivity — difficulty distinguishing faces from backgrounds or objects against similar-coloured surfaces — occurring even before measurable acuity loss.
Impaired dark adaptation, meaning prolonged difficulty adjusting vision after moving from a bright to a dim environment, an early functional marker of RPE dysfunction.
Colours appearing less vivid or washed out, particularly yellows and blues, reflecting cone photoreceptor damage in the central macula.
Need for brighter light to read or perform close tasks, with standard ambient lighting no longer sufficient as the disease advances.
Difficulty recognising faces at a distance, progressing to inability to see the speaker's face in conversation, even when peripheral navigation remains intact.
New-onset visual disturbance in one eye, often asymmetric, with the fellow eye frequently showing early or intermediate changes on examination.
earlyWarningSigns: Occasional blurring of central vision that clears, lasting seconds to minutes initially.
Needing more light when reading than a year ago.
Straight lines looking very slightly wavy on the Amsler grid when one eye is covered.
Colours looking slightly different between the two eyes when viewed separately.
emergencySigns: Sudden, painless loss of central vision in one eye within hours — suggests new-onset neovascular AMD, a retinal vascular event, or subretinal haemorrhage requiring same-day ophthalmic assessment.
Rapid distortion or a dark patch in the centre of vision developing over 24-48 hours.
Loss of vision accompanied by a dark curtain descending — may indicate retinal detachment, a separate emergency.
Visual field loss with eye pain, halos around lights, or a red eye — may indicate acute angle-closure glaucoma.

causes.description: Cellular waste products and lipid-rich deposits (drusen) accumulate between the RPE and Bruch's membrane with age, impairing the RPE's ability to nourish and maintain photoreceptors, creating a chronic low-grade inflammatory state that drives progressive photoreceptor death.

Genetic variants in complement factor H (CFH), complement factor B, and C3 reduce the eye's ability to suppress chronic local complement activation, allowing persistent sub-RPE inflammation to damage the RPE and Bruch's membrane — the strongest known molecular pathway in AMD pathogenesis.

The macula is exposed to high levels of light and oxygen consumption, generating reactive oxygen species over decades that damage RPE cells, lipid membranes, and mitochondrial DNA; this oxidative burden accumulates as antioxidant defences decline with age.

In genetically susceptible individuals with established drusen and RPE stress, upregulation of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) drives ingrowth of fragile new blood vessels from the choroid beneath the retina; these vessels leak plasma and blood, rapidly damaging the overlying photoreceptors.

Cigarette smoking doubles the risk of AMD by generating substantial oxidative stress, impairing choroidal blood flow, reducing levels of protective carotenoids in the retina, and promoting thickening of Bruch's membrane — making it the strongest modifiable environmental risk factor.

The ARMS2 and HTRA1 loci confer a 2- to 3-fold increased risk per risk allele and interact with smoking to further amplify AMD susceptibility, affecting mitochondrial function and extracellular matrix remodelling in the RPE and choriocapillaris.

riskFactors.description: AMD risk rises exponentially with age: prevalence roughly doubles every decade from age 55. Over 25% of people over 80 have some form of AMD, compared with less than 1% below age 55.

Active smoking approximately doubles the risk of AMD relative to non-smokers, and the risk persists for years after cessation. Passive smoke exposure also elevates risk, making it the single most important modifiable risk factor.
First-degree relatives of AMD patients have a 3- to 6-fold elevated lifetime risk. The CFH Y402H and ARMS2 A69S variants together explain 50-65% of AMD's population-attributable genetic risk.
AMD is considerably more common in people of European descent than in those of African or East Asian ancestry; darker macular pigment in people of African descent may offer partial protection.
Hypertension, atherosclerosis, and high BMI are associated with increased AMD risk, likely through shared mechanisms of choroidal vascular insufficiency, oxidative stress, and lipid deposition.

Diets low in leafy vegetables, lutein, zeaxanthin, and omega-3 fatty acids are linked to higher AMD risk; the AREDS and AREDS2 trials showed supplementation reduces late AMD progression in intermediate and advanced disease.

riskFactors.factor: Light iris colour and prolonged UV exposure
riskFactors.description: Blue or grey irises allow more ultraviolet and short-wavelength light to reach the retina; cumulative UV exposure is a hypothesised contributor to RPE oxidative damage, though evidence is weaker than for smoking.
BMI above 30 kg/m² is associated with a roughly 2-fold increase in neovascular AMD risk in some cohort studies; regular aerobic exercise is associated with lower AMD incidence in prospective data.

affectedDemographics: Adults over 55, with risk increasing sharply each decade
People with a first-degree family member with AMD
Individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or obesity
Women, who have a somewhat higher lifetime burden due to greater longevity
diagnosisOverview: AMD is diagnosed by comprehensive ophthalmologic examination. The key finding on dilated fundus examination is the presence of drusen — yellow-white sub-RPE deposits of varying size — along with any pigmentary changes, areas of geographic atrophy, or signs of choroidal neovascularisation such as subretinal fluid, haemorrhage, or exudate. Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is the most important imaging modality: it provides cross-sectional views of the retinal layers at micron-scale resolution, revealing drusen, subretinal fluid, intraretinal fluid, pigment epithelium detachments, and geographic atrophy with precision unavailable to fundoscopy alone. OCT-angiography (OCT-A) allows non-invasive visualisation of the retinal and choroidal vasculature, detecting neovascular networks without dye injection, though fluorescein angiography remains the gold standard for characterising neovascular activity and lesion boundaries in equivocal cases. Fundus autofluorescence imaging tracks RPE metabolic health and maps the progression of geographic atrophy over time. Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) and low-luminance visual acuity quantify the functional impact. An Amsler grid is a simple home-monitoring tool that can detect new distortion (metamorphopsia) prompting urgent review. AMD staging uses the validated Age-Related Eye Disease Study (AREDS) or the international simplified classification. Genetic testing for CFH and ARMS2 variants is used in research and occasionally to counsel first-degree relatives but does not change management in most clinical settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Direct visualisation of drusen, pigmentary changes, geographic atrophy, haemorrhage, and neovascular membranes by a retinal specialist.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Dilating drops are placed in both eyes, causing temporary blurred near vision and light sensitivity for 4-6 hours. The doctor examines the retina using a slit lamp and specialised lenses or an indirect ophthalmoscope.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Cross-sectional imaging of all retinal layers to quantify drusen volume, detect subretinal or intraretinal fluid, assess geographic atrophy boundaries, and monitor treatment response in wet AMD.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You rest your chin on a support and look at a small light while the instrument scans your retina with near-infrared light. The scan takes about 5 minutes, causes no discomfort, and requires no dye injection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps RPE metabolic activity and lipofuscin accumulation; areas of hypo-autofluorescence delineate geographic atrophy and guide prognosis and enrolment in clinical trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A retinal photograph taken with a specialised blue-light camera after dilation; takes about 2 minutes and requires no injection.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Intravenous dye-based imaging that characterises neovascular lesion type, extent, and activity when OCT is inconclusive and prior to photodynamic therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fluorescein dye is injected into an arm vein; photographs are taken over 10-15 minutes as the dye moves through retinal vessels. Mild nausea and yellow skin discolouration lasting a few hours are common. Rare allergic reactions can occur.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive visualisation of choroidal neovascular networks for diagnosis and monitoring without fluorescein or indocyanine green dye.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same process as standard OCT but with a slightly longer scan; no injection required.

diagnosticTests.test: Best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) and Amsler grid
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies functional vision loss and detects metamorphopsia; tracked at every visit and used as the primary endpoint in treatment trials.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You read a letter chart with your best corrective lens in place; the Amsler grid is a printed 10 cm × 10 cm grid viewed at 30 cm to check for wavy or missing lines.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment strategy depends on the AMD stage and subtype. There is no currently approved therapy for early AMD; lifestyle modification (smoking cessation, exercise, diet) and monitoring are advised. For intermediate AMD and for one eye with late AMD, the AREDS2 formula supplement — 500 mg vitamin C, 400 IU vitamin E, 10 mg lutein, 2 mg zeaxanthin, 80 mg zinc oxide, 2 mg cupric oxide daily — reduces the 5-year risk of progression to advanced AMD by approximately 25% in the target population and represents the standard of care. Geographic atrophy now has two complement-inhibiting treatments approved by the FDA: pegcetacoplan (Syfovre) and avacincaptad pegol (Izervay), both given as intravitreal injections every 1-2 months; they reduce the rate of geographic atrophy growth by roughly 20% but do not reverse existing atrophy or restore lost vision.

Neovascular (wet) AMD is treated primarily with intravitreal anti-VEGF agents — ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab (off-label), brolucizumab, and faricimab — injected directly into the vitreous cavity every 4-16 weeks depending on disease activity. Major trials (MARINA, ANCHOR, VIEW 1 & 2) showed that monthly ranibizumab and aflibercept stabilised or improved vision in approximately 90-95% of patients, with roughly 30-40% gaining 15 or more Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) letters. Faricimab, a bispecific antibody targeting VEGF-A and Ang-2, permits extended dosing intervals up to every 4 months in approximately half of patients. Low-vision rehabilitation — magnification aids, eccentric viewing training, and lighting optimisation — is integral to management at all late stages and should be offered alongside medical treatment. Photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin retains a role in specific subtypes such as pure occult neovascularisation and polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy.

medicalTreatments.name: AREDS2 nutritional supplement formula
medicalTreatments.description: A specific daily combination of lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc oxide 80 mg, and cupric oxide 2 mg, replacing the original AREDS beta-carotene formula. Indicated for patients with intermediate AMD in one or both eyes, or late AMD in one eye. Not needed or proven for early AMD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces risk of progression to advanced AMD at 5 years by approximately 25% vs placebo in the AREDS2 trial (N Engl J Med, 2013), with no significant benefit from beta-carotene or additional omega-3 supplementation.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal ranibizumab (Lucentis)
medicalTreatments.description: A VEGF-A fragment antigen-binding (Fab) antibody fragment injected into the vitreous to suppress choroidal neovascularisation in wet AMD. Approved for monthly or as-needed (treat-and-extend) dosing. A standard first-line agent in many national formularies.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stabilised or improved BCVA in approximately 94-95% of patients vs 62% with sham injection in the pivotal MARINA and ANCHOR trials; mean gain of approximately 7 letters at 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal aflibercept (Eylea)
medicalTreatments.description: A fusion protein blocking VEGF-A, VEGF-B, and placental growth factor, injected intravitreally. After 3 monthly loading doses, maintenance can be extended to every 8 weeks, reducing injection burden compared to monthly ranibizumab while maintaining equivalent efficacy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to monthly ranibizumab in the VIEW 1 and VIEW 2 trials, with approximately 95% of patients maintaining vision at 52 weeks; every-8-week dosing after loading matches monthly dosing.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravitreal faricimab (Vabysmo)
medicalTreatments.description: A bispecific antibody simultaneously targeting VEGF-A and angiopoietin-2 (Ang-2), enabling longer dosing intervals (up to every 16 weeks) in roughly half of patients in real-world extended dosing protocols.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to aflibercept in the TENAYA and LUCERNE trials, with approximately 45% of patients eligible for 16-week intervals at year 1, reducing annual injection burden by 4-5 injections compared to every-8-week dosing.

medicalTreatments.name: Pegcetacoplan (Syfovre) — geographic atrophy
medicalTreatments.description: A C3 complement inhibitor injected intravitreally every month or every other month; the first FDA-approved treatment for geographic atrophy in dry AMD (2023). Does not restore lost vision but slows atrophy growth.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduced geographic atrophy lesion growth rate by approximately 22% vs sham at 24 months in the OAKS and DERBY trials; treatment-related endophthalmitis rate approximately 0.2% per injection.

medicalTreatments.name: Low-vision rehabilitation and assistive devices
medicalTreatments.description: Optical magnifiers, electronic magnification systems (video magnifiers, digital tablets), screen-reading software, eccentric viewing training to use remaining peripheral retina, and home adaptation for lighting and contrast. Provided by low-vision specialists and occupational therapists.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured low-vision rehabilitation improves reading speed and task performance; approximately 50-70% of AMD patients benefit measurably from eccentric viewing training in randomised studies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mediterranean diet adherence
alternativeTreatments.description: A diet rich in oily fish (omega-3 fatty acids), leafy green vegetables (lutein and zeaxanthin), whole grains, and olive oil is associated with 20-40% lower risk of AMD progression in prospective cohort studies, complementing AREDS2 supplementation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin
alternativeTreatments.description: Verteporfin is activated by low-energy laser to selectively close neovascular vessels. Largely superseded by anti-VEGF for typical wet AMD but still used for polypoidal choroidal vasculopathy and occult lesions where anti-VEGF response is incomplete.

alternativeTreatments.name: Home Amsler grid monitoring
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily self-monitoring with an Amsler grid allows early detection of new metamorphopsia signalling conversion from dry to wet AMD; prompt presentation for injection can preserve vision that would otherwise be lost.

whySeeSpecialist: A retinal specialist or medical retinologist should be involved for any patient with intermediate or advanced AMD, any new visual symptoms suggesting wet AMD, or any patient being considered for anti-VEGF therapy or geographic atrophy treatment. Retinal specialists have access to OCT, OCT-A, and angiography; they perform intravitreal injections and can monitor treatment response with sensitivity not available in primary eye care. Early referral to low-vision rehabilitation prevents unnecessary disability as the disease progresses.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a fellowship-trained medical retinal specialist with a specific interest in AMD and access to spectral-domain OCT, OCT-A, and a full injection suite. Ask about their approach to treat-and-extend protocols versus fixed-interval dosing — the protocol affects injection burden significantly. For geographic atrophy, seek a centre participating in clinical trials of complement-pathway therapies. For low-vision referral, request a certified low-vision optometrist or rehabilitation specialist alongside retinal care.
preventionStrategies: Stop smoking — it is the single most potent modifiable risk factor, approximately doubling AMD risk.
Take AREDS2 supplements if you have intermediate AMD in one or both eyes, or late AMD in one eye; do not take them for early AMD without specialist advice.
Eat a Mediterranean-style diet high in dark leafy greens, oily fish, and antioxidant-rich foods.
Wear UV-protective sunglasses outdoors, particularly in high UV environments.
Control blood pressure and cardiovascular risk factors through lifestyle and, where indicated, medication.
Maintain a healthy body weight and exercise regularly — at least 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week.
Have annual dilated eye examinations after age 55, or more frequently if intermediate AMD is diagnosed.
Monitor the Amsler grid daily at home if you have intermediate AMD, and seek urgent review for any new distortion.
lifestyleModifications: Quit smoking — cessation reduces future AMD progression risk, even after decades of smoking.
Increase dietary intake of lutein (kale, spinach, corn) and zeaxanthin (orange peppers, goji berries).
Increase oily fish consumption to at least twice weekly for omega-3 fatty acids.
Use adequate, directed task lighting for reading; reduce glare with anti-reflective lenses and window shading.
Download the NHS or NOF Amsler grid app for daily, consistent home monitoring.
Learn to use low-vision aids early before vision deteriorates further — adaptation is faster when started earlier.
Inform driving authorities if visual acuity or visual field falls below legal driving thresholds.

recommended: Dark leafy greens (kale, spinach, chard) for lutein and zeaxanthin content
Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) at least twice weekly for omega-3 fatty acids
Orange and yellow vegetables and fruits for carotenoids
Nuts and seeds for vitamin E
Legumes and whole grains to support cardiovascular health and healthy BMI
AREDS2 supplement formula for intermediate or late-stage AMD
avoid: Smoking, which depletes macular carotenoids and causes oxidative damage
High-glycaemic-index foods, which are associated with increased AMD risk in some cohorts
Excess saturated and trans fat, which adversely affect choroidal circulation
Beta-carotene supplements (original AREDS formula) in smokers, due to increased lung cancer risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — brisk walking, cycling, or swimming for at least 150 minutes per week — is associated with reduced AMD incidence and progression in prospective cohort studies, likely through improved choroidal perfusion, lower BMI, and reduced cardiovascular risk. Exercise does not worsen AMD or pose eye-specific risks. As vision declines, a physiotherapist or low-vision specialist can advise on fall prevention and safe exercise environments.
dailyManagement: Check the Amsler grid with one eye covered each morning; seek same-day urgent care if new distortion or a new blind patch appears.
Take AREDS2 supplements consistently at the prescribed dose — do not double up.
Keep injection appointments without postponement; missed anti-VEGF injections commonly lead to vision loss in wet AMD.
Ensure home lighting is bright and directed — a 100-watt equivalent LED desk lamp for reading tasks.
Use a tablet or e-reader at maximum brightness and font size to reduce reliance on deteriorating central acuity.
Discuss driving safety honestly with your ophthalmologist; legal thresholds exist to protect both the patient and others.
Connect with a low-vision support group or patient organisation (e.g. Macular Society UK, Macular Degeneration Association US) for peer support and equipment advice.
prognosis: Prognosis depends critically on AMD subtype and stage at diagnosis, and on treatment access for wet AMD. Early AMD rarely progresses to vision-impairing disease: annual conversion rate to late AMD is below 1%. Intermediate AMD carries a 5-year risk of advancing to late AMD of roughly 18% without supplementation and approximately 13% with AREDS2. Geographic atrophy progresses slowly over years but inevitably impairs central vision; mean enlargement rate is approximately 1.8 mm² per year, and most patients with bilateral late geographic atrophy lose reading ability within 5-10 years of foveal involvement. Wet AMD, if untreated, causes severe central vision loss in 50% of eyes within 2 years; with anti-VEGF treatment, approximately 30-40% of patients gain meaningful vision and 90-95% maintain baseline vision at 12 months. Long-term (10-year) outcomes are more guarded: treatment burden is high, approximately 10-15% of treated eyes still lose 15 letters or more over 7 years in real-world data, and bilateral involvement is common. Peripheral vision is generally preserved, enabling independent mobility even with advanced disease. Low-vision rehabilitation substantially offsets the functional impact and should not be delayed until vision is severely reduced.
recoveryTimeline: AMD is a chronic condition with no complete recovery. With anti-VEGF treatment for wet AMD, most patients see their best visual response within the first 3-6 monthly injections (the loading phase); gains stabilise or consolidate over 12 months. Many patients shift to a treat-and-extend protocol thereafter, with injections spaced out every 6-8 weeks or longer if the retina remains dry. Dry AMD with geographic atrophy progresses slowly over years; new complement-inhibiting agents slow but do not halt progression. Patients should expect lifelong monitoring and, for wet AMD, ongoing injections at whatever interval disease activity requires.
complications: Severe central vision loss — the defining complication of late AMD; affects reading, face recognition, and driving.
Subretinal haemorrhage — bleeding under the retina from neovascular vessels that can acutely devastate central vision within days if untreated.
Disciform scar — end-stage wet AMD produces a fibrovascular scar in the macula that permanently destroys the overlying photoreceptors.
Depression and anxiety — clinically significant in approximately 20-30% of patients with bilateral advanced AMD, linked to activity limitation and loss of independence.
Falls and injury — impaired depth perception and contrast sensitivity from AMD increase fall risk by 2- to 3-fold in older patients.
Endophthalmitis — a serious intraocular infection that can occur in approximately 0.05-0.1% of anti-VEGF injections and requires urgent treatment to prevent permanent vision loss.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Facts About Age-Related Macular Degeneration
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information from the U.S. National Institutes of Health on AMD types, causes, diagnosis, and treatment.

supportResources.name: RNIB — Macular degeneration
supportResources.description: UK patient guide from the Royal National Institute of Blind People, including practical living tips and benefit guidance.

supportResources.name: Macular Society UK
supportResources.description: UK charity offering helpline, peer support groups, and information on treatments and low-vision aids for AMD.

sources.title: Age-Related Eye Disease Study 2 (AREDS2) Research Group — Lutein/Zeaxanthin for the Treatment of Age-Related Cataract. JAMA Ophthalmol, 2013
Wong WL et al. Global prevalence of age-related macular degeneration. Lancet Glob Health, 2014
Rosenfeld PJ et al. Ranibizumab for neovascular age-related macular degeneration (MARINA trial). N Engl J Med, 2006
Heier JS et al. Intravitreal Aflibercept (VEGF Trap-Eye) in Wet Age-related Macular Degeneration (VIEW 1 and VIEW 2). Ophthalmology, 2012
NICE TA800 — Faricimab for treating wet age-related macular degeneration (2022)
National Eye Institute — Age-Related Macular Degeneration: Data and Statistics
Holz FG et al. Pegcetacoplan treatment for geographic atrophy due to AMD (OAKS/DERBY trials). Ophthalmology, 2023

confusedWithConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diabetic retinopathy occurs in people with diabetes and involves microaneurysms, neovascularisation throughout the retina, and both central and peripheral vision loss, whereas AMD is unrelated to blood glucose and affects exclusively the central macula.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma damages the optic nerve and causes peripheral visual field loss first, with central vision typically preserved until late disease, whereas AMD selectively destroys central vision while peripheral vision remains intact.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataracts cause uniform blurring of the entire visual field from lens clouding and are completely correctable by surgery, whereas AMD causes a permanent central scotoma not correctable by any lens or cataract operation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A visual cortex stroke causes abrupt homonymous visual field defects affecting the same side in both eyes, whereas AMD is slow in onset (dry form) or more acute unilaterally (wet form) and confined to the central macula.

relatedConditions.name: Diabetic retinopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Both affect the retina and can cause macular oedema; anti-VEGF treatment is used in both, and patients with diabetes may develop both conditions concurrently.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Glaucoma and AMD are the two leading causes of irreversible blindness in older adults and frequently coexist, complicating both diagnosis and intravitreal injection management.

relatedConditions.name: Cataracts
relatedConditions.relevance: Cataracts are nearly universal in the AMD age group; cataract surgery can unmask or worsen AMD symptoms and requires careful pre-operative AMD assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Shared vascular risk factors (hypertension, smoking, atherosclerosis) predispose to both stroke and AMD; a transient ischaemic attack or stroke can mimic or coexist with AMD-related vision loss.

faqs.question: What is the difference between dry and wet AMD?
faqs.answer: Dry AMD involves slow accumulation of drusen deposits under the retina and gradual atrophy of retinal cells over years; it accounts for 85-90% of cases. Wet AMD occurs when abnormal blood vessels grow under the retina and leak fluid, causing faster and more severe central vision loss. Dry AMD can convert to wet AMD at any time, which is why regular monitoring matters.

faqs.question: Can AMD cause total blindness?
faqs.answer: AMD destroys central vision but almost never causes complete blindness because peripheral vision is preserved. Even with advanced bilateral AMD, most people retain enough side vision to navigate independently. However, loss of central vision can prevent reading, driving, and recognising faces, profoundly affecting quality of life.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of macular degeneration?
faqs.answer: The earliest sign is often mild blurring of central vision or a need for brighter light to read. Straight lines appearing wavy or distorted when viewed with one eye covered (tested with an Amsler grid) is a key early warning of wet AMD. In dry AMD, early disease is usually asymptomatic and found on routine eye examination.

faqs.question: Do AREDS2 supplements work for all types of AMD?
faqs.answer: AREDS2 supplements are proven to reduce progression to advanced AMD by about 25% in people with intermediate AMD in one or both eyes, or late AMD in one eye only. They have not been shown to benefit people with early AMD or no AMD, and do not treat wet AMD. Take them only if your eye specialist has confirmed you are in the target group.

faqs.question: How often do intravitreal injections need to be given?
faqs.answer: For wet AMD, most patients receive three loading injections given monthly, then switch to a treat-and-extend schedule where the interval is lengthened by 2 weeks at each visit if the retina stays dry. Many patients stabilise on 8-12 week intervals; newer agents like faricimab allow intervals up to 16 weeks in suitable patients. Stopping injections too early commonly leads to vision loss.

faqs.question: Are intravitreal injections painful?
faqs.answer: Most patients report the injections as much less uncomfortable than expected. The eye is numbed with anaesthetic drops first; the injection itself takes a few seconds. Many patients feel a pressure sensation rather than pain. A brief ache or blurry vision for a few hours afterwards is common. Serious complications such as infection or retinal detachment are rare but possible.

faqs.question: Can stopping smoking help if I already have AMD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stopping smoking at any stage reduces the rate of AMD progression and lowers the risk of conversion from dry to wet AMD. Smokers with AMD who quit have better visual outcomes than those who continue. Smoking cessation is the single most important lifestyle step a person with AMD can take.

faqs.question: Is AMD hereditary?
faqs.answer: AMD has a strong genetic component. Having a parent or sibling with AMD increases your lifetime risk 3- to 6-fold. Variants in the CFH and ARMS2 genes explain a substantial proportion of this genetic risk. However, AMD is not simply inherited in a Mendelian fashion; lifestyle factors such as smoking, diet, and UV exposure interact with genetic susceptibility.

faqs.question: Can AMD be detected before symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Yes. Drusen and early RPE changes are visible on dilated fundus examination or OCT before any visual symptoms occur. This is why annual eye examinations from age 55 are recommended, particularly for people with a family history of AMD or who smoke. Early detection allows timely supplementation and closer monitoring.

faqs.question: What happens if wet AMD is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated neovascular AMD leads to severe irreversible central vision loss in approximately half of affected eyes within 2 years. The leaking vessels eventually scar the macula, producing a fibrovascular scar called a disciform scar. Once the photoreceptors overlying the scar are destroyed, vision loss at that location is permanent. Early treatment with anti-VEGF injections is critical.

faqs.question: Can I drive with AMD?
faqs.answer: Driving with AMD depends on the degree of vision loss. You must meet the legal visual acuity and visual field standards in your country. Many people with early or intermediate AMD can continue driving; those with advanced bilateral AMD generally cannot meet the legal standard. You have a legal obligation to inform your licensing authority and insurer if AMD affects your vision to the required degree.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for AMD?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for AMD at present. Anti-VEGF injections control but rarely cure wet AMD, and most patients require ongoing treatment indefinitely. For geographic atrophy, newly approved complement inhibitors slow progression but do not reverse lost vision. Gene therapy trials targeting AMD are underway and show early promise. Low-vision rehabilitation can substantially improve function despite the lack of a cure.

faqs.question: What foods should I eat or avoid to protect my macular health?
faqs.answer: Eat plenty of dark leafy greens (kale, spinach) for lutein and zeaxanthin, oily fish twice weekly for omega-3 fatty acids, and a variety of colourful vegetables and fruits. Avoid smoking, which depletes macular pigment. If you already have intermediate AMD, take the AREDS2 supplement formula. Avoid high-glycaemic foods. A Mediterranean-style diet is associated with lower AMD progression risk.

faqs.question: How is AMD monitored over time?
faqs.answer: AMD is monitored with dilated fundus examination and OCT at intervals determined by disease stage: typically annually for early AMD, every 6 months for intermediate AMD, and every 4-8 weeks for patients receiving anti-VEGF treatment. Home Amsler grid monitoring is used daily by patients with intermediate or late AMD to detect sudden changes between clinical visits.

faqs.question: What is an Amsler grid and how do I use it?
faqs.answer: An Amsler grid is a 10 cm × 10 cm grid of evenly spaced horizontal and vertical lines with a dot in the centre. Cover one eye, hold the grid 30 cm away, focus on the central dot, and note whether any lines appear wavy, blurry, or missing. Switch to the other eye. Any new change from your baseline — especially new waviness or a new blank patch — should prompt same-day contact with your eye specialist.

faqs.question: Does AMD affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: AMD is a bilateral disease, but the two eyes are usually affected asymmetrically — one eye often has more advanced disease than the other. When one eye develops wet AMD, the risk of the fellow eye developing wet AMD is approximately 10-12% per year. This is why the follow eye is monitored closely even when only one eye requires injection treatment.

faqs.question: What new AMD treatments are being developed?
faqs.answer: Several promising treatments are in clinical trials, including gene therapy to deliver sustained anti-VEGF production with a single subretinal injection (reducing the need for repeat injections), stem cell-based RPE transplantation, and additional complement-inhibiting drugs for geographic atrophy. Artificial intelligence tools to predict AMD progression from OCT scans are also being validated for clinical use.

faqs.question: Can low-vision aids help people with AMD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even when medical treatment cannot restore lost vision, low-vision rehabilitation can dramatically improve daily function. Options include handheld and stand magnifiers, electronic video magnifiers, large-print and audio books, screen-reading software for computers and tablets, and eccentric viewing training — learning to use peripheral retina to replace the damaged central area. Referral to a low-vision specialist should not be delayed until vision is severely reduced.

faqs.question: How does AMD affect mental health?
faqs.answer: Depression and anxiety affect approximately 20-30% of people with bilateral advanced AMD, linked to loss of independence, inability to drive, and difficulty recognising faces. These rates are significantly higher than in age-matched controls without vision loss. Regular screening for depression in AMD follow-up, and referral to mental health support or peer groups, is an important part of care.

metaTitle: Macular Degeneration: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: AMD causes central vision loss from retinal damage in adults over 50. Learn about dry vs wet AMD, AREDS2 supplements, anti-VEGF injections, and prevention.
